walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Main Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get The Castles of Burgundy set up. First, place the large game board in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. This board has a lot going on, but do not worry, we will walk through each section. You will see six numbered depots around the edge, a central black depot, a victory point track running around the border, a turn-order track, and five phase spaces labeled A through E.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Hex Tiles by Color
walkthrough.text: Now find all one hundred sixty-four hex tiles and sort them into two groups based on their backs. Most tiles have colored backs matching their front color. Sixteen tiles have black backs instead. Separate the black-backed tiles into their own face-down stack. Sort the remaining colored-back tiles into face-down stacks by color: beige for buildings, light green for animals, yellow for knowledge, dark green for castles, grey for mines, and blue for ships.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the General Supply
walkthrough.text: Place the twenty Silverling tokens in a supply pile near the board. These are the game's currency. Next to them, place all thirty worker tiles in another supply pile. Workers are how you adjust your dice, so you will be grabbing these frequently. Finally, set the white die near the board. The starting player will roll this die each round.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Goods Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find all forty-two square goods tiles. They come in six different colors. Shuffle them all face down. Now make five face-down piles of five goods tiles each and place one pile on each of the five phase spaces labeled A through E on the board. Set the remaining seventeen goods tiles face down as a reserve supply off to the side.

walkthrough.title: Place the Bonus Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the twelve bonus tiles. There are two for each of the six tile colors: one large bonus tile and one small bonus tile per color. Place each bonus tile on its matching space on the game board. These are claimed by the first and second players to fill all estate spaces of a given color, so they create a race throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Depots Based on Player Count
walkthrough.text: This step depends on how many players you have. Each of the six numbered depots has four hex spaces, and each space is marked with a player-count number. For two players, fill only the spaces marked with a two, which gives you twelve total hex tiles on the board. For three players, fill spaces marked two and three for eighteen tiles total. For four players, fill all twenty-four spaces. Draw tiles randomly from the colored-back stacks, matching each depot space's color.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Black Depot
walkthrough.text: Now fill the central black depot with random tiles drawn from the black-backed stack. The number of tiles depends on your player count. Place them face up so everyone can see what is available. The black depot gives players access to tiles outside the normal numbered depots.

walkthrough.title: Set Round Goods
walkthrough.text: Take the five goods tiles from the Phase A pile and place them face up on the five round spaces on the board. Each round, the starting player will move one of these goods tiles to a depot based on the white die roll. This is how goods enter the depots throughout each phase.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Boards
walkthrough.text: Give each player one player board, also called an estate board. For your first game, everyone should use Estate number one, which is the same on all four copies. The board shows thirty-seven colored hex spaces arranged in regions of connected same-color spaces. You will be filling these spaces with hex tiles throughout the game. Each space has a number from one to six printed on it, which tells you what die value you need to place a tile there.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Castle and Goods
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one dark-green castle tile and places it on the dark-green space marked with a six in the center of their estate board. This is your starting castle. Then deal three random goods tiles from the reserve supply to each player. Stack goods of the same color together and keep different colors separated in your goods storage area.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Player Pieces
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes two dice and two playing pieces in that color. Place one playing piece on the zero space of the victory point track around the main board. Place the other playing piece on the turn-order track. Also take one personal one hundred and two hundred victory point marker and keep it beside your board for when you lap the track.

walkthrough.title: Give Out Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one Silverling from the supply and places it in their Silverling storage on their player board. Then distribute worker tiles based on turn order. The starting player gets one worker, the second player gets two, the third player gets three, and the fourth player gets four. This compensates for going later in the first round.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Everyone rolls their two dice. The player with the highest total goes first and receives the white die. Place all turn-order markers on the first space of the turn-order track, with the starting player's marker on top and the last player's on the bottom. The stacking order matters because it determines who goes first when players share a space.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: Your table should now have the main board with filled depots, bonus tiles displayed, and goods on the round spaces. Each player has an estate board with a starting castle, three goods tiles, dice, one Silverling, and their worker tiles. You begin in Phase A, Round one. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: The Castles of Burgundy is played over five phases, labeled A through E. Each phase contains five rounds, for a total of twenty-five rounds. At the start of each phase, the board's depots are refilled with fresh hex tiles, and new goods tiles are laid out on the round spaces. The game ends after round five of Phase E.

walkthrough.title: What Happens Each Round
walkthrough.text: At the start of every round, all players roll their two personal dice simultaneously. The starting player also rolls the white die. The starting player then takes the top goods tile from the round track and places it on whichever numbered depot matches the white die value. Then players take turns in turn-order sequence, with each player performing exactly two actions, one per die.

walkthrough.title: Turn Order Matters
walkthrough.text: Turn order is determined by the turn-order track on the main board. Players who are further to the right go first. If multiple players share the same space, the player whose marker is on top of the stack goes first. This becomes important because certain tiles and actions let you move along this track, giving you first pick of the depots before other players.

walkthrough.title: Your Four Action Options
walkthrough.text: On your turn you have four possible actions, and you must perform exactly two of them using your two dice. You can take a hex tile from a depot, place a hex tile from your storage into your estate, sell goods for victory points and Silverlings, or buy a tile from the black depot using Silverlings. You may take the same action twice if you wish. Let's walk through each one.

walkthrough.title: Action One: Take a Tile from a Depot
walkthrough.text: If your die shows a three, you pick from depot three. Choose any tile in that depot regardless of its color. Place the tile in one of your three personal storage slots at the bottom left of your estate board. If all three slots are full, you must discard one stored tile first to make room.

walkthrough.title: Action Two: Place a Tile into Your Estate
walkthrough.text: Use one of your dice to place a tile from your personal storage into your estate. The die value must match the number printed on the estate space where you want to place the tile. The tile's color must match the space's color. And the space must be adjacent to at least one tile you have already placed, including your starting castle. When you place a tile, its effect triggers immediately.

walkthrough.title: Action Three: Sell Goods
walkthrough.text: Use one of your dice to sell all goods of one color from your goods storage. You do not need to match the die value to anything specific for this action. You score victory points for each goods tile sold, and you also receive one Silverling per sale action. The number of victory points per tile depends on your player count: two points per tile with two players, three with three players, and four with four players.

walkthrough.title: Action Four: Buy from the Black Depot
walkthrough.text: Use one of your dice to buy a tile from the central black depot. This costs two Silverlings regardless of which tile you take. You do not need to match the die value to the depot. The tile goes into your personal storage, just like when you take from a numbered depot. This is useful when none of the numbered depots have what you need.

walkthrough.title: Worker Tiles and Dice Adjustment
walkthrough.text: Worker tiles are your safety net for bad dice rolls. You can spend one worker tile to adjust one of your dice by plus or minus one. The adjustment wraps around, so a six can become a one and a one can become a six. You can spend multiple workers on the same die to shift it by several steps. Workers cannot be used to adjust the white die. Gaining extra workers is always useful because it gives you more flexibility.

walkthrough.title: Placing Buildings
walkthrough.text: When you place a beige building tile into your estate, its unique effect triggers immediately. There are many different building types, and each one does something different. Some give you workers or Silverlings, some let you take a tile from a depot, and some score victory points. Buildings are extremely diverse, so check the reference card in your edition for the full list.

walkthrough.title: Placing Ships
walkthrough.text: When you place a blue ship tile, two things happen. First, you take all the goods tiles from any one depot's goods space and add them to your goods storage. You can hold up to three different colors of goods at once. Second, you move your turn-order marker one space to the right on the turn-order track, placing it on top of any markers already there. If this makes you the new first player, you receive the white die.

walkthrough.title: Placing Animals
walkthrough.text: When you place a light-green animal tile into a pasture on your estate, you score victory points equal to the number of animals shown on the tile, which ranges from two to four. But here is the powerful part. If there are already animal tiles of the same type in that connected pasture, you also rescore every matching animal tile in that pasture. Animals in different pastures do not count, only the ones in the same connected group.

walkthrough.title: Placing Castles
walkthrough.text: When you place a dark-green castle tile, you immediately get one bonus action as if you had a die showing any value you choose. This is incredibly powerful because it essentially gives you a free third action on your turn. You can use this bonus action to take a tile, place a tile, sell goods, or buy from the black depot. Castle tiles are rare and valuable.

walkthrough.title: Placing Knowledge Tiles
walkthrough.text: Yellow knowledge tiles provide ongoing advantages or special abilities. Some modify your actions for the rest of the game, like letting you adjust dice without spending workers. Others give you bonus victory points at the end of the game based on certain conditions. Each knowledge tile has unique text, so read it carefully when you place it. Knowledge effects are powerful because they compound over many rounds.

walkthrough.title: Placing Mines
walkthrough.text: When you place a grey mine tile, you gain one Silverling at the start of every future round for the rest of the game. This does not include the round you placed it. Mines are an economic engine that rewards early placement. A mine placed in Phase A will generate many more Silverlings over the course of the game than one placed in Phase D.

walkthrough.title: Completing Regions
walkthrough.text: Your estate is divided into colored regions of connected same-color spaces. When you fill every space in a region, you score bonus victory points. The smaller the region, the fewer points, but smaller regions are easier to complete early. Completing a region in an earlier phase also gives you more points than completing the same-sized region later. This means there is a strong incentive to finish regions as quickly as possible.

walkthrough.title: Claiming Bonus Tiles
walkthrough.text: When you complete all spaces of a single color across your entire estate, you claim a bonus tile from the board. The first player to finish all spaces of a color takes the large bonus tile, worth more victory points. The second player to finish that same color takes the small bonus tile, worth fewer points. Once both tiles are claimed, no further bonus is available for that color. This creates an exciting race between players.

walkthrough.title: Phase Transitions
walkthrough.text: After the fifth round of each phase, the phase ends. All remaining hex tiles are cleared from the numbered depots and returned to the box. However, goods tiles sitting on depot goods spaces stay put. The depots are refilled with new hex tiles from the colored-back stacks, the black depot gets fresh black-backed tiles, and the next phase's five goods tiles are placed on the round spaces. Then a new phase begins.

walkthrough.title: The Goods Storage Limit
walkthrough.text: You can hold goods tiles in up to three different color groups in your goods storage at any time. If you already have three colors and gain goods of a fourth color, you cannot take them. Plan your ship placements and goods sales carefully so you do not get locked out of valuable goods. Selling a color to clear storage space before picking up new goods via ships is a common and important tactic.

walkthrough.title: End of Game and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after Phase E, Round five. All victory points from tile placements, goods sales, region completions, and bonus tiles have already been scored during play, so there is no complex end-of-game calculation. Simply check if any players have passed one hundred or two hundred on the victory point track using their personal marker. Some knowledge tiles may provide additional end-game scoring. The player with the most victory points wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Loire Valley
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted series of turns with three example players, each making different choices and sometimes different mistakes. Follow along with the moves even if your player count differs. By the end, you will have seen every action type and understand how the game flows.

walkthrough.title: Phase A Begins
walkthrough.text: It is Phase A, Round one. The depots are stocked with fresh hex tiles, and five goods tiles are sitting on the round spaces. Every player rolls their two dice. Player one, as the starting player, also rolls the white die. The white die shows a four, so Player one places the first round's goods tile onto depot four. Now the turns begin.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Taking a Tile
walkthrough.text: Player one rolled a three and a five. They use the three to take a ship tile from depot three. The ship goes into one of their three personal storage slots. Then they use the five to take a building tile from depot five. Both storage slots now hold tiles waiting to be placed into the estate on future turns. Notice how Player one did not place anything yet. Sometimes drafting tiles first is the smart move.

walkthrough.title: Why Drafting First Works
walkthrough.text: New players often feel pressured to place tiles immediately, but storage gives you flexibility. By drafting tiles this round, Player one can spend workers next round to adjust dice for perfect placement. The two-stage flow of drafting then placing is central to this game. Think of your storage as a planning bench.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: First Placement
walkthrough.text: Player two rolled a four and a six. They already have an animal tile in storage from a previous thought experiment, so they use the six to place it onto an empty light-green space marked six on their estate, adjacent to the starting castle. The tile shows three cows, so Player two scores three victory points immediately. That is how animal scoring works: points equal the animals shown on the tile.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Second Action
walkthrough.text: With the remaining four, Player two takes a knowledge tile from depot four and stores it. Good move. Knowledge tiles provide ongoing benefits, and getting them early means more rounds to use their effects. Player two is building a foundation of flexibility.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: The Mistake of Overdrafting
walkthrough.text: Player three rolled a two and a five. They already have two tiles in storage. Instead of placing one first, they use both dice to draft two more tiles from depots two and five. But wait. Their storage only has three slots, and now they have four tiles. Player three must discard one of their stored tiles to make room. That discarded tile is gone forever.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Draft with Purpose
walkthrough.text: The lesson here is simple: do not hoard tiles without a placement plan. Your three storage slots are precious. Every tile you draft should have a clear destination in your estate within the next round or two. If you draft blindly, you will be forced to discard tiles you worked to acquire, and that is pure waste.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: The White Die and Goods
walkthrough.text: A new round begins. Everyone rolls dice again, and Player one rolls the white die. It shows a one, so the next goods tile goes to depot one. This is important because ships let you grab all the goods from a depot. Players who have been watching the goods placement now know where the juicy goods are stacking up.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Placing a Ship
walkthrough.text: No problem. They spend one worker tile to adjust the three up to a four, then place the ship onto the blue space marked four in their estate. Two things happen immediately. First, Player one takes all the goods tiles from one depot goods space of their choice. Second, their turn-order marker moves one space to the right.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Splitting Animals Across Pastures
walkthrough.text: Player three places a cow tile into a light-green pasture near the edge of their estate, scoring two victory points. Next round, they place another cow tile. But this time they put it in a different pasture region on the opposite side of the board because the die value happened to match that space. The second cow tile scores its own points, but it does not trigger the powerful rescore of the first cow tile because they are in separate pastures.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Keep Same Animals Together
walkthrough.text: Pasture rescoring is one of the biggest point swings in the game. When you add a cow tile to a pasture that already has cows, you rescore every cow tile in that pasture. Three cow tiles in one pasture score far more than three cow tiles spread across three separate pastures. Always plan your animal placement so same types stay connected.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Using a Castle Tile
walkthrough.text: Immediately, they get a free bonus action with any die value they choose. Player two uses this bonus action to place a knowledge tile from storage that they could not otherwise reach with their rolled dice. That is three actions in a single turn. Castles are incredibly powerful because of this flexibility.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Neglecting Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Several rounds have passed. Player one has placed two ships and is now first in turn order, getting first pick of every depot. Player two placed one ship and sits in second position. Player three has placed zero ships. They are stuck in last position every round, consistently getting the leftover tiles that nobody else wanted. The quality of their estate is suffering.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Ships Control the Tempo
walkthrough.text: Turn order is not just a tiebreaker. The player who goes first gets the best tile selection every single round. Ships are the only way to advance on the turn-order track, and they also give you goods for selling later. Ignoring ships means you are always picking from leftovers. Try to place at least one ship early to stay competitive in turn order.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Selling Goods
walkthrough.text: They use one die to sell all four orange goods at once. In a three-player game, each goods tile is worth three victory points, so Player one scores twelve victory points and gains one Silverling. That is a massive scoring burst from a single action. Goods sales are one of the most efficient point-scoring actions in the game.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Wasting Workers on Low-Impact Adjustments
walkthrough.text: Player three has been spending workers almost every round to adjust dice by one or two. They used a worker to take a building they did not really need. They used another worker to grab a mine in a phase where it will barely pay off. Now they are out of workers entirely, and they just rolled double twos when they desperately need a five to complete a region before the phase ends. No workers, no adjustment, no completion bonus.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Conserve Workers for Critical Moments
walkthrough.text: Workers are a finite and valuable resource. Spending them on minor convenience adjustments leaves you helpless when a critical placement opportunity appears. Save your workers for moments that matter: completing a region for bonus points, placing a castle for a free action, or grabbing a contested tile before an opponent. Think of workers as insurance, not spare change.

walkthrough.title: Completing a Region
walkthrough.text: Player two has been steadily filling a three-space yellow knowledge region. With their last placement, they complete the region in Phase B. The game awards bonus victory points for completing regions, and completing them earlier in the game gives more points. Player two earns a healthy chunk of points. On top of that, if Player two is the first to complete all yellow spaces across their entire estate, they will claim the large yellow bonus tile.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Completing a Color
walkthrough.text: They claim the large blue bonus tile from the board, worth significant victory points. Player three was also working on blue but finishes second and takes the smaller bonus. This is the race element in action. Once the large tile is claimed, it is gone forever.

walkthrough.title: Phase Transition
walkthrough.text: Phase A ends after its fifth round. All remaining hex tiles are swept off the numbered depots and returned to the box. Goods tiles already sitting on depot goods spaces stay. The depots are refilled with fresh tiles from the supply stacks, and the Phase B goods are placed on the round spaces. The game resets for a new phase while your estate progress carries forward.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Checkpoint
walkthrough.text: By the middle of the game, you should have a rough plan for which regions you can realistically complete and which colors you might finish first for bonus tiles. Your estate is filling up, and adjacency becomes easier because you have more placed tiles to connect to. This is when knowledge tiles and mines really start paying off because their ongoing effects have had time to accumulate.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Remember: draft with purpose, keep same animals in the same pasture, do not ignore ships and turn order, and save your workers for moments that really count. You are ready to play a full game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Build Your Estate
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and pacing advice across the five phases.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to tile types, building effects, and scoring rules.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

bullets: First in turn order = best tile selection every round
Ships advance turn order AND grant goods

bullets: Finishes all blue spaces → claims large blue bonus tile
Player three finishes 2nd → gets smaller bonus

bullets: Phase ends → clear leftover hex tiles, keep depot goods
Refill depots + black depot with fresh tiles

bullets: Seen all 4 actions, 3 common mistakes, key game rhythms

Ready to play a full game

summary.title: Before You Build Your Estate
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics and phase pacing
Reference the Appendix → tile types, buildings, scoring rules
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, let's address the traps that catch new players. First, overdrafting tiles into storage without a placement plan leads to painful discards. Second, scattering same-type animals across different pastures instead of consolidating them kills your rescoring potential. Third, ignoring ships and turn order means you are always picking from leftovers. Fourth, burning through worker tiles on trivial adjustments leaves you helpless when a critical placement window appears. Keep these in mind and you will leapfrog most beginners.

walkthrough.title: Phase-by-Phase Pacing
walkthrough.text: Each phase has its own rhythm. In Phase A, focus on getting your infrastructure started. Place a ship for turn-order advantage, grab a mine for long-term Silverling income, and start filling small regions. Phases B and C are your building phases. Place knowledge tiles for ongoing benefits, consolidate animal pastures, and push to complete regions before bonuses are claimed. Phases D and E are the endgame sprint. Focus on selling goods, finishing remaining regions, and placing high-value animal tiles for massive rescore chains.

walkthrough.title: Worker Tile Management
walkthrough.text: Workers are one of the most important resources in the game, and new players undervalue them. Every worker you spend on a trivial adjustment is one you cannot spend later on a critical placement. The best approach is to stockpile workers during the early phases and spend them strategically in the mid and late game when specific placements yield large point swings. Gain extra workers whenever you can through buildings and knowledge tiles that provide them.

walkthrough.title: The Goods Economy
walkthrough.text: Goods scoring scales with player count, making them more valuable in larger games. The key is to stack goods of the same color and sell them in bulk for maximum efficiency. Ships are the only way to acquire goods after your initial three, so plan your ship placements around depots that have goods sitting on them. Selling also clears goods storage space, which matters because you can only hold three different colors at once. Do not let a full storage prevent you from taking valuable goods via ships.

walkthrough.title: Region Completion Timing
walkthrough.text: Completing regions earlier in the game awards more victory points per space. A three-space region completed in Phase A is worth more than the same region completed in Phase D. This means you should prioritize small, achievable regions early to bank guaranteed points. Larger regions naturally take more time, so plan your tile drafting and dice adjustments around completing them before the phase changes. Also remember that completing all spaces of one color triggers the bonus tile race, so consider which color you can finish first.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Board State
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what other players are doing. If someone is one tile away from completing a color, they are about to claim a bonus tile before you. If goods are stacking up on a particular depot, someone with a ship is about to grab them. If an opponent is low on workers, they cannot adjust dice to reach contested tiles. Use this information to prioritize your own moves. Sometimes taking a tile you do not need is worth it if it denies a huge score to an opponent.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Scoring Sources
walkthrough.text: The highest-scoring players earn points from every available category. Animal rescoring, goods sales, region completions, bonus tiles, and building and knowledge effects all contribute. Tunneling on just one strategy, like only placing animals, leaves points on the table from other sources. The estate board is designed so that you must place multiple tile types anyway, so lean into each type's scoring potential rather than treating some as filler.

walkthrough.title: The Silverling Economy
walkthrough.text: Silverlings let you buy tiles from the black depot, which is your escape valve when the numbered depots do not have what you need. Mines generate one Silverling per round for the rest of the game, making early mine placement extremely valuable. Goods sales also grant one Silverling per transaction. Keep a small reserve of Silverlings available so you can react to strong black-depot tiles when they appear. Do not hoard them either. Two Silverlings spent on the perfect tile is always a good trade.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't overdraft tiles — 3 storage slots, discard if full
Don't scatter same animals across different pastures
Don't ignore ships — turn order = first pick of depots
Don't burn workers on trivial adjustments — save for critical moments

bullets: Phase A: infrastructure — ship, mine, small regions
Phases B-C: building — knowledge, animals, push region completions
Phases D-E: sprint — sell goods, finish regions, high-value animal rescoring

Ships = only way to gain goods after initial 3
Goods storage: 3 colors max — sell to clear space before new pickups
VP per tile: 2P=2, 3P=3, 4P=4

summary.title: Region Completion Timing
bullets: Earlier phase → more VP per region space
Prioritize small regions in Phase A for guaranteed early points
Plan drafting + dice adjustment around phase deadlines
First to complete a full color → large bonus tile

summary.title: Reading the Board State
bullets: Watch opponents' near-complete colors for bonus tile races
Track goods stacking on depots — ship timing matters
Opponents low on workers = can't reach contested tiles

summary.title: Diversify Your Scoring Sources
bullets: Points from: animals, goods, regions, bonuses, buildings, knowledge
Don't tunnel on one type — estate forces mixed placement anyway
Lean into each tile type's scoring potential

summary.title: The Silverling Economy
bullets: Black depot = escape valve when numbered depots lack what you need
Mines: 1 Silverling/round for rest of game — place early
Goods sales grant 1 Silverling per transaction
Keep a small reserve, but spend 2 for the perfect tile

walkthrough.title: The Animal Recursion Engine
walkthrough.text: Animal pastures offer the most explosive scoring potential in the game. When you add a cow tile to a pasture that already contains cows, you rescore every cow tile in that pasture. A fourth cow tile in a single pasture triggers a rescore of all four tiles, yielding a massive point burst. The key is planning your estate layout so that all light-green animal spaces in a region can be filled with the same animal type. Target large connected pastures and be patient about waiting for matching animal tiles rather than placing mismatched ones early.

walkthrough.title: Building the Animal Engine
walkthrough.text: To maximize animal recursion, draft animal tiles selectively and store them until you can place them into the right pasture. Use workers aggressively to adjust dice for animal placements since each successful same-type addition compounds the scoring. Knowledge tiles that grant extra victory points per animal tile or that let you treat different animals as the same type are extremely valuable enablers. In a best-case scenario, four or five same-type animals in one pasture can score upwards of thirty-five to forty points from rescoring alone.

walkthrough.title: Ship-First Tempo Strategy
walkthrough.text: Placing ships in Phase A gives you two immediate advantages: goods for future sales and turn-order priority. Being first in turn order for the entire game means you always get the best tile from every depot. This compounds over twenty-five rounds into a significant quality advantage for your estate. The goods you collect via ships are also a reliable victory point source when sold. Target a ship placement on your very first turn if the dice cooperate.

walkthrough.title: Maximizing Turn-Order Advantage
walkthrough.text: The compounding advantage of first pick is subtle but enormous. You can deny critical tiles from opponents, cherry-pick the exact building or animal tile you need, and control the pace of the bonus tile race. Combine ship placements with goods sales for a double benefit: advance your position while building toward a bulk sale worth twelve or more victory points.

walkthrough.title: Mine and Knowledge Engine
walkthrough.text: Mines and knowledge tiles are the economic backbone of a control-oriented strategy. A mine placed in Phase A generates one Silverling per round for approximately twenty remaining rounds. Two early mines give you a steady stream of Silverlings to buy from the black depot whenever you need a specific tile. Knowledge tiles that reduce worker costs, grant bonus points, or modify placement rules create ongoing advantages that compound over the game's twenty-five rounds. This strategy trades early scoring for late-game flexibility and efficiency.

walkthrough.title: Building Synergy Chains
walkthrough.text: Beige building tiles have diverse effects, and the best players chain them together. A building that grants a free tile take, followed by placing a castle for a bonus action, followed by placing another building that grants workers, creates a cascading turn of three or four effective actions. The key is reading the available building types in the depots and planning your placements so that each building's immediate effect sets up the next. Castle tiles are the linchpin because their free bonus action can trigger placement chains.

walkthrough.title: The Bonus Tile Race
walkthrough.text: Bonus tiles reward the first and second players to complete all spaces of a single color. The large bonus tile is worth five to seven victory points depending on player count, which can be game-deciding. Identify which color you can realistically complete first and dedicate resources to that race. Small regions of a rare color like dark green or grey are often easier to complete because they have fewer total spaces. Watch your opponents' boards to see who is close to completing a color and decide whether to race them or pivot to an uncontested color.

walkthrough.title: Dice Mitigation Mastery
walkthrough.text: Expert players rarely feel constrained by their dice because they plan for mitigation. Store tiles that match multiple potential die values. Keep two or three workers in reserve so you can always reach a critical space. Use knowledge tiles that provide free die adjustments to stretch your workers further. The best turns often start with a seemingly useless roll that gets transformed by a worker into the exact value needed to complete a region, trigger an animal rescore, or claim a castle's bonus action. Dice are random, but mitigation is a skill.

4th cow in a pasture rescores all 4 tiles = massive burst
Plan estate layout so animal spaces connect into large pastures
Be patient — wait for matching animals rather than placing mismatches

entries.definition: One of five major periods in the game, labeled A through E. Each phase consists of five rounds. At the start of each phase, depots are refilled with new hex tiles and goods tiles are placed on round spaces.
One cycle within a phase where all players roll dice, goods are distributed via the white die, and each player takes two actions. There are five rounds per phase and twenty-five rounds total.

Each player's personal board containing thirty-seven colored hex spaces arranged in regions. Spaces are numbered one through six, indicating the die value needed for placement. Estate number one is recommended for first games; boards two through nine offer alternative layouts.
A group of connected same-color spaces on your estate board. Completing all spaces in a region awards bonus victory points, with earlier completion yielding more points.

One of six numbered areas on the main board, each holding hex tiles and a goods space. Players use dice to take tiles from the depot matching their die value. Depots are refilled at the start of each phase.
The central depot on the main board filled with black-backed hex tiles. Tiles here can be purchased for two Silverlings using any die value, bypassing the normal die-matching requirement.

A track on the main board that determines play sequence each round. Players further right go first. Ties are broken by marker stacking, with the top marker going first. Ships advance your position on this track.
Three slots on each player's estate board for holding drafted hex tiles before placement. If all three are full and you draft a new tile, you must discard one first.
When placing a hex tile into your estate, it must be adjacent to at least one previously placed tile. Your starting castle counts for adjacency. This rule shapes your expansion path across the board.
A hex tile can only be placed on an estate space of the same color. Beige buildings go on beige spaces, blue ships on blue spaces, and so on.

entries.term: Take a Tile from a Depot
entries.definition: Use one die to select a numbered depot matching the die value. Take any one hex tile from that depot and place it in your personal storage. If storage is full, discard one stored tile first.

entries.term: Place a Tile into Your Estate
entries.definition: Use one die to place a tile from your personal storage onto a matching-number, matching-color estate space adjacent to an already-placed tile. The tile's effect triggers immediately upon placement.
Use one die of any value to sell all goods tiles of one color from your goods storage. Score victory points per tile based on player count, and gain one Silverling per sale action.
Use one die of any value and pay two Silverlings to take any tile from the central black depot. The tile goes into your personal storage.

Beige hex tiles that trigger a unique immediate effect when placed. Effects vary widely and include gaining workers, Silverlings, taking tiles from depots, scoring victory points, and more. Check your edition's reference card for the complete list of building types.
Blue hex tiles that provide two immediate benefits when placed: take all goods from one depot's goods space, and advance your turn-order marker one space to the right on the turn-order track.

Light-green hex tiles placed in pastures. Score victory points equal to the number of animals shown on the tile. If the same animal type already exists in that connected pasture, all matching tiles in that pasture are rescored.

Dark-green hex tiles that grant one immediate bonus action using any die value of your choice. This effectively gives you a free third action on your turn. Castle tiles are rare and extremely powerful.

Yellow hex tiles providing ongoing advantages or end-game scoring conditions. Effects include free die adjustments, bonus points for certain tile types, or modified placement rules. Their value compounds over many rounds.

Grey hex tiles that generate one Silverling at the start of every future round for the rest of the game. Does not include the round the mine was placed. Earlier placement yields significantly more total Silverlings.
A beige building that lets you sell one type of goods immediately upon placement, as if taking a sell action. Useful for converting goods into points without spending a die on selling.
A beige building that lets you immediately take one beige building tile from any numbered depot and place it into your storage. Can chain into further building placements.
A beige building that lets you immediately take one castle, mine, or knowledge tile from any numbered depot and place it into your storage.
A beige building that lets you immediately take one animal or ship tile from any numbered depot and place it into your storage.
A beige building that lets you immediately take one tile of any type from the black depot without paying Silverlings.
A beige building that grants two Silverlings immediately when placed. Helps fuel black depot purchases.
A beige building that lets you immediately place one tile from your storage into your estate, as if you had a die of any value. The placed tile triggers its normal effect.
A beige building that scores four victory points immediately when placed. One of the most straightforward buildings in the game.

Filling all spaces in a colored region on your estate awards bonus victory points. Smaller regions give fewer points. Completing a region in an earlier phase awards more points than the same region completed later.

Twelve tiles on the main board, two per hex color. The first player to complete all estate spaces of one color claims the large bonus tile. The second player claims the small bonus tile. Point values scale with player count.
The large bonus tile awards five victory points in a two-player game, six in a three-player game, and seven in a four-player game.
The small bonus tile awards two victory points in a two-player game, three in a three-player game, and four in a four-player game.

When selling goods, each tile is worth two victory points with two players, three with three players, and four with four players. You also receive one Silverling per sale action regardless of the number of tiles sold.

When placing an animal tile into a pasture that already contains the same animal type, all matching animal tiles in that connected pasture are rescored for their full value. Animals in different pastures do not trigger rescoring.

The scoring track around the main board running from zero to one hundred. When a player passes one hundred, they flip their personal marker to the two hundred side. Some games see scores exceeding two hundred.

The game's currency token. Used primarily to buy tiles from the black depot at a cost of two Silverlings. Gained through mines, goods sales, certain buildings, and initial setup. Each player starts with one.

Tokens used to adjust die values by plus or minus one per worker spent. Adjustments wrap around from six to one and one to six. Multiple workers can be spent on the same die. Cannot be used on the white die.

A shared die rolled by the starting player each round. Its value determines which numbered depot receives the current round's goods tile. Cannot be adjusted with workers and cannot be used as an action die.

Square tiles in six colors. Three are dealt to each player at setup, and additional goods enter depots via the white die each round. Collected through ship placements and sold for victory points and Silverlings.
Two dice in each player's color, rolled at the start of every round. Each die is used for one action per round. Die values can be adjusted with worker tiles.

A connected group of light-green animal spaces on your estate board. Same-type animals placed in the same pasture trigger rescoring. Animals in separate, disconnected pastures do not interact for scoring purposes.
Players can hold goods tiles in up to three different color groups. If you already hold three colors, you cannot take goods of a fourth color until you sell one group to free space.
If all three personal storage slots are occupied and you draft a new hex tile, you must discard one stored tile to make room. Discarded tiles are removed from the game permanently.
When adjusting dice with worker tiles, values wrap around. A six adjusted up by one becomes a one, and a one adjusted down by one becomes a six. This costs one worker per step.
At the end of each phase, all remaining hex tiles are removed from numbered depots and returned to the box. Goods tiles on depot goods spaces remain. Depots are then refilled for the next phase.
In three-player games, the dark-green space in depot six uses a castle tile in phases A, C, and E, and a mine tile in phases B and D. This alternating pattern is unique to three-player games.

A dark-green castle tile placed on the center die-six space of each player's estate during setup. This is the anchor point for adjacency. Unlike normal castle placements, it does not grant a bonus action.
